metadata.career_relevance: 'Group anagrams' appears in the first technical phone screen at virtually every Indian tech company. Zerodha, Razorpay, CRED all ask frequency-based string problems in screening rounds.

hook.real_world_problem:
```
Zerodha ticker normalization:
Are 'ICICI' and 'ICICI' the same? Yes — trivial.
Are 'silent' and 'listen' anagrams? Yes — same frequency!

Sorting approach: O(k log k) per string
Frequency approach: O(k) per string + O(26) comparison

For 10M strings: frequency wins decisively
```
hook.what_you_will_build: Anagram checker, group anagrams, find all anagrams in string (sliding window).

visual_aid.description: Group anagrams diagram. Input: ['eat','tea','tan','ate','nat','bat']. Process: sort each word as key. 'eat'→'aet', 'tea'→'aet', 'tan'→'ant', 'ate'→'aet', 'nat'→'ant', 'bat'→'abt'. HashMap: 'aet'→[eat,tea,ate], 'ant'→[tan,nat], 'abt'→[bat]. Output: [[eat,tea,ate],[tan,nat],[bat]]
visual_aid.alt_text: Group anagrams using sorted key HashMap

worked_example.scenario: Find All Anagrams in a String (LeetCode #438).

step_by_step_solution.details: Window size = p.length()=3. Compare int[26] freq arrays. O(26)=O(1) comparison. Slide: add right char, remove leftmost char.

worked_example.final_code: See exercises — fixed window with int[26] comparison.
things_to_notice: int[26] comparison: O(26) = O(1) constant — much faster than HashMap
Arrays.equals() on int[26]: clean idiom for frequency comparison
Fixed window: add nums[i], remove nums[i-k] — same sliding window pattern as T30.2

industry_examples.use_case: Find duplicate merchant IDs that are anagrams of each other (possible fraud detection). Group anagrams O(n×k) where k=ID length.

applications.interview_relevance: Anagram patterns: (1) check → int[26] freq array O(n), (2) group → sort as key O(n×k log k) or freq as key O(n×k), (3) find all in string → fixed sliding window O(n).
applications.real_world_workflow: Default to int[26] for lowercase alpha (O(1) space and comparison). Use HashMap<Character,Integer> for arbitrary character sets (Unicode). Sorted key for grouping when O(k log k) is acceptable.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using HashMap<Character,Integer> when int[26] would work (lowercase alpha only). HashMap: O(1) average but with hash overhead. int[26]: O(1) constant, O(1) space comparison with Arrays.equals().
common_gaps.remediation: Rule: lowercase 'a'-'z' → int[26]. Uppercase+lowercase → int[128]. Any Unicode → HashMap. int arrays are always faster for fixed-size character sets.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Student uses HashMap for lowercase-only anagram problem — correct but suboptimal

key_takeaways: int[26]: O(1) space and O(1) comparison for lowercase alpha
Group anagrams: sort key O(k log k) or freq array key O(k)
Find all anagrams: fixed window + int[26] comparison O(n)
int[26] comparison: always char-'a' for lowercase
Never HashMap when int[26] works — faster, simpler, O(1) space

self_check_questions.question: Why use int[26] instead of HashMap<Character,Integer> for anagram problems?
self_check_questions.answer: Three reasons: (1) Speed — int[] array access is direct memory offset O(1) with no hashing overhead; (2) Space — int[26] is exactly 26 integers = 104 bytes, constant regardless of input; (3) Comparison — Arrays.equals(freq1, freq2) is O(26) = O(1) constant time. HashMap comparison requires O(k) where k is the number of unique characters. For lowercase alpha problems, int[26] is always the right choice.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: String DP — Longest Common Subsequence, Edit Distance, and Longest Palindromic Subsequence.
